sentences.text: So we just-- you know, as I say, we were just-- we find that hut.
And we couldn't-- we couldn't, you know, let the fire extinguish.
And then I was hunting for food with my other friend.
That, it lasted, you know, for three weeks.
And then we did it, and then-- and after, you know-- No, we stayed there for nearly two weeks first.
And then, you know, every day, it was my task to do everything.
The other people, they were cold, they were old, they were sick.
So it was my friend and myself to collect the snow, to hunt for food, do everything.
But they were just looking after the fire.
So one morning, early in the morning, while I was collecting, you know, the snow into the pail, you know, I find in front of my pail, two big boots, men boots.
And then I was afraid the German has come back myself.
I said, my gosh.
And I look it up.
And I saw a stranger uniform, said that is not a German's.
And then, I imagine I say-- and then they couldn't speak my language.
They couldn't speak English.
They couldn't speak French.
They couldn't speak nothing.
And they say, you-- no, not they say you because they didn't know English either.
I say, Russian, [NON-ENGLISH].
He say, yes, you guess it, it's Russia.
And then, I took by hand and say-- I couldn't speak the language.
I took by hand to show him there is not only me, there are eight people in that hut.
I came, you know, with this Russian.
I came, you know, there, toward the hut.
And I say to everybody, Russian.
And I collapsed.
And I passed away.
The first time, you know, in the camp, by the joy, by the wind, I don't know what it was, I pass away.
You know, I become unconscious.
I just collapse.
Russian, I say, and everybody was, you know, so happy.
Me, I collapse, the first time I collapse, you know, in the thing.
And the poor fellow, you know, he had in his pocket-- I don't know, I still remember today, he had like cubes of sugar in his pocket and lots of almonds.
[NON-ENGLISH] He took all his sugar and all his almonds, he gave it to us, just, you know, to-- to-- like a gift.
He had nothing.
And he told us, you know, that try to make thing that you should go so many kilometers away.
There is a camp, he explained to us, plenty Jewish, plenty soldier.
They're all Russian there.
We have to be there.
We should go there.

So the next day-- And then, while we were in that cart, we will see clearly all the fighting, the Russian against the Allied, you know, in the snow, in the sky, all these, you know, fires, and bombs, and this, and that.
We were not even scared.
We were looking in the sky.
So-- but in any case, when we heard also in the camp, the Russian is there.
And they are going to look after us.
We didn't trust.
I say, well, maybe it must be another kind of Germans or whatever, if it has to be a camp.
So we won't go.
We decide all of us not to go, to stay there where we are.
And that was because we can hunt plenty food by that time.
But that time, we found with my friend lots and lots of deposit of mineral water.
And we don't even have to melt the snow anymore.
That Vichy water.
I don't know, the German, they're full, full, of holds full of boxes of Vichy water, because Vichy is the best water you can have, like mineral water.
So we can-- we were carried those, with the sleds, into our thing.
Food in abundance, we find tins, tins, and tins, and say, why we have to move.
We are happy here.
We don't go nowhere.
We are wait here, you know, until all this fighting is over, because we knew it was still fighting.
And we stayed there for three and a half weeks.
That, you know, the first week we saw the Russian.
We stay another two and a half weeks there, not even thinking of going away from there.
I don't know.
We were stupid, because what-- how can you stay in the middle of the forest with nobody to look after you?
Eventually, you know, one girl-- another girl of ours, she was in that camp.
In that camp.
And she was also looking around because they can-- they were free to go here and there.
She come to this hut where we were hiding.
And she told us, you know, we are there, not so bad.
Not only that, say they are starting sending, you know, Italians with the Italian, Germans with the Germans, to be together.
Not that the war is finished, but because the war is still going on until April and May.
But it was only the end-- the beginning of February.
So you must come with everybody else.
Otherwise, who will look after you?
You will-- you'll die all the same here.
So they say, you must come.
We had no belonging to take with or whatever.
We took whatever food we had with us.
And we went where-- she explained to us where they were staying.
And it was, you know, a few miles away.
We arrived there.
And there was a big camp of refugee like us, you know, from Auschwitz, that they put together from other places.
You know, they were all there.
They-- Maybe they find some other one in Auschwitz.
I don't know.
But they were there.
And the doors were closed.
And the sentry said, what do you want.
Say, it was somebody, you know, who could speak little bit of French.
And we say, there.
We couldn't speak like that.
And he went to say to the commandant, you know, the commandant of the camp.
He say, no way.
They're not allowed to come in.
Because we are saying that the group, we didn't know, I think, that they were Germans-- No, he say you could-- you know, they make us very difficult to go in.
And we start crying.
What can we do?
Say, I don't know.
Whatever you want to do, you are not allowed.
Maybe you are--
Maybe you were friends of the German.
Maybe you are partisan.
I don't know what you are.
But you are not allowed to enter the camp.
And we were very desperate.
In that very moment, you know, a general, a Russian general-- I don't know what is the name.
But I knew it at the time.
I have forgot.
He came.
And he was speaking perfect French, which we speak French.
We explained the matter to him.
And he went inside.
He was a general, because the commandant of the camp, it was just a small-- a small officer, whatever.
But he was one of the generals of the army of the Russian that he was, you know, for the Allied, you know, to-- to make this liberation of the camp.
And he spoke to them.
And plus, beside him, there was a very important person in the camp who was a Frenchman.
And he was an important man.
And he was Huberman, Huberman, the violinist.
He also, you know, intervened-- you know, intervened for us.
And he spoke to the commandant.
Eventually, he let us in, because-- but at one condition.
When did this camp form, at the time, you know, there were many, many, many prisoners-- I mean, like you say, like us.
But they were sick with-- with tuberculosis.
So they say, you will come in the camp at the condition you are going to nurse those people sick with tuberculosis.
And we say, doesn't matter.
We lose-- we didn't know what if it was dangerous or not dangerous, but so long that they let us in, we say we would look after the tuberculosis people.
Don't worry.
And we went there.
But after a day or two, there was another Frenchman.
He was a doctor.
He was a doctor.
He went to speak-- you know, because he was a doctor in this hospital-- in this-- it was not hospital.
It was not hospital.
It was like a block of sick people.
He went to speak to the commandant.
He say, definitely, I'm not allowed, those girls, these girls to come and work in this tuberculosis.
Because weak as they are, and being, you know, you know, nearly, you know very sick themselves, it's very easy to catch tuberculosis themselves as well.
So I better put, you know, like proper, you know, soldiers or something to look after those people.
And they are not even trained nurse or anything.
Then know how to-- the hygiene or whatever it is.
So the commandant of the camp say, all right, you will not do, you know, tuberculosis, you know.
But you will peel potatoes.
I said, that is-- that's very nice.
And then we were there for a few weeks.
It was in Bialystok, Bialystok, which is also at the frontier with Poland.
So in the meantime, in this camp, beside the Jews, they had also concentrated, you know, other, like, other prisoner, political, gypsies, whatever it is.
And then it was we doing nothing-- now, waiting, waiting the war to be finished.
And then some, you know, they want to pass their time, you know.
And they were making, like every night, you know, for all these prisoners, they were making like shows there.
That was the war do.
We were sick.
We were dizzy.
We were making shows.
And then, I don't know.
One of my friend told-- told another there of these people, they must take me also for the show.
I can sing songs in Spanish and in Italian.
And, eventually, the general came one night to hear these people making the shows.
And he liked my song.

Every night after that, until, you know, we moved away, he will come for half an hour to hear just my Spanish-- my Spanish song, which was 'Caminito.' And then, in reward, to reward me there in the camp, he will give me double ration of food, which the food consisted with the Russian, kasha in the morning with a little bit of sugar.
Kasha is wheat, boiled wheat.
That, it was breakfast.
Lunchtime, the same kasha with salt and haricot beans.
And at night, kasha and haricot beans.
Day in, day out, day in, day out.
They say that, in Russia, everybody eats the same.
But it was not true, because we saw the officer.
They were eating meat fricadelles, and chops, and this and that.
But the soldier and us, we was kasha.
Kasha is the main, like you say, the macaroni for the Italians.
So my reward for singing at night for the general was to have double kasha for lunch, for breakfast, and for dinner, which I was sharing with my friends there, with me.
Because even that we were know they giving enough portion, but it was still, you know, not enough.
We were just sharing.
And then, we were staying in that.
And they move us to another camp.
Another camp, it was I think Bialyk itself.
Bialyk and Bialystok, they are nearly more or less nearby.
There, we stay, you know, like two months, two months in the other thing.
And they were taking us, the girls, to go and clean the-- the places where the Russians were, you know, to clean the floor like servants, you know.
And then we didn't like that, because if we were free, why we have to go there and clean those places?
And, eventually, they told us, other people prisoner, they were there now.
We find they're also Italian soldiers.
You know, they were also there in that place.
They were saying to us, you know, we heard that they are not going to send us back to Italy.
They are going to send us all to Siberia.
To Siberia?
Said, no, we don't want to go to Siberia.
And then what we can do?
So those two, three partisans, they were partisans.
That's why they were in the camp.
They were caught in Italy while, you know, they were in the-- in the-- in the fields.
They were partisans.
With a group of us, which it was our four, five women-- the two French girls, and the Spanish, and the whatever, and the old man what it was-- it was limping.
We were all together.
You know, say, we'll try to escape to Europe someplace.
So it was a chaos in Europe, unbelievable.
There, you know, everybody was going all direction-- no passport, no tickets, no this.
You don't believe what was happening those trains.
So we went to the station.
We saw a train going.
And we jump in.
No, it was during the morning time.
It was, you know, wires in the camp.
But it was not electric wire.
There was just wire.
So these Italian people, you know, the two partisan, they make a hole into the-- into the-- into the wire, and we escape.
We escape from that place.
I don't know what happened to the other prisoner.
Maybe they were sent to Siberia, because many people that, you know, witness the liberation, they say they saw with their own eyes, they'd been liberated, some of them.
But they never came to their destination.
So where are they?
Where are they gone?
And we don't know what's happening.
So we, young kids, we escaped with this.
We had-- the-- you know, the luck that we find those Italian that speak Italian.
And we were-- we-- we find those Italian that speak Italian.
And they tell us, you know, where to go and what to do.
Otherwise, we were going to remain there maybe in Siberia today.
So in any case, we find this train going.
We don't know where it was going.
Come the shifter and he say, tickets.
And we show our number.
Said, that is our tickets.
He let us go in the-- in the train.
And we were just, you know, traveling, traveling, traveling got a day and the night.
And then the train came to an end, stopped.
We have to come down the train.
And we saw another train going.
So we jump into the other train.
And after a day and the night, we arrive exactly the place where we have left, you know.
Again, back.
We didn't know where, because we didn't know the language.
It was like Polish.
It was Russian.
I don't know what it was.
We didn't know a word.
So, eventually, you know, we find another train that it was going full of Americans.
Full of Americans, they know a few words and everything.
But they were in another train.
They said, take that train.
It's going to-- to south.
You know, it's going to south of Europe.
We took that train.
Again, the same thing.
We had no food or ID.
We were showing the numbers to go into the train.
But they couldn't give us any food.
Whenever the train was stopping here and there, we will go and pinch, and steal food.
We were passing through all travel.
We would steal-- they were the beautiful.
It was September.
It was beautiful cherries in the trees.
We were taking apples, whatever you found, just-- and that is what we ate, you know, during the train.
There was no food for us.
And then, you know, the train went to Prague, went to Budapest.
It went to all the places it was stopping.
But, I must tell you, wherever we went, we found the Red Cross very-- the Red Cross, they were always waiting in the trains for-- they don't know who was passing.
Whoever was passing, they were little bit of basket to give an apple or so to this prisoner.
And, eventually, we arrived to a place, you know, that there were all the Italians there, going to Italy, the prisoner, Italians.
Not only that, the other Jewish-- Jewish people that had escaped from other camps ready to go to Italy.
In the meantime, it was already September.
The war had already finished, you know, a few months.
So we joined this thing.
And we went through the Brenner Road.
Brenner Road is through the Alps.
Like you say, the Alps in Italy, we went through the Brenner Road.
And we arrive in Italy.
We arrived to Italy because we were meant to go to Rhodes Island, back where we came.
We didn't know where to go.
And then, to go-- the Brenner Road is in the Italy of north.
To go to Rhodes Island, we have to go all day Italy until it comes to the south, to take a boat to go to Rhodes Island, because you cannot go-- it's an island.
So when we were taking, you know, these trains with the contingents of the refugees, they stop in Bologna.
And they put us there, you know, for temporary, for a couple of nights.
While we were there-- and then we were telling our story that we-- because there were thousands of refugees that from one town of Italy going to another town, they bombarded their own houses.
There were shelters all broken up.
And they were going all the direction to join the relatives-- one to the north, one to the south.
And then we told our story that we were not like them, you know.
We were different, these four people.
And we thought at the time we were the only survivors of the Auschwitz contingent.
We didn't know what's happen to the other one.
We thought we are only three people out of 2,000.
And we don't know what to do.
And then these people that speak Italian, they were all Italian, say, listen, the best thing for you, you are not Catholic.
You are Jewish people.
There is a community we know in Bologna.
There is a community of Jewish.
You must go and speak to them, see what they tell advice to you.
So we advised-- you know, we arrived there.
And they give us the direction.
We went by foot.
In the meantime, while I was in, you know, with the Russian, my clothes, they were all, you know, torn and broken.
I had exchanged them.
The Russian has given me-- because I was singing, they were giving me a uniform of Russian, you know, soldier.
I was-- it was September.
It was very hot in Italy, Bologna especially.
There is no sea there.
I had boots in-- like you say, in fur, you know, like the Russian.
It was very, very hot.
And then, I was in khaki, and in hat.
It was like I was like a Russian.
And then, when I was starting going to go toward the community, all the [NON-ENGLISH], all the small children, they were following me.
They thought I was a Russian soldier, you know.
They were all seeing what kind of a person I am to be a soldier.
Eventually, we arrive at the Jewish community.
We went to the-- to the president of the Jewish community of Bologna, which eventually, later on, we stayed there for a year.
They with very, very good to us.
They were like, you know-- you know, they were-- they didn't know what to do.
Not only them, the whole community, they were trying their best to help us.
And then, I'll tell you just now.
In any case, we arrived there.
And then they explained to us, you say, we are the only survivor.
We have lost our relative.
We have lost all the Jewish.
Say, you know what?
You've got here in this same building upstairs, this house belonged before, previous to the war, it belongs to the-- to the rabbi, the rabbi of Bologna.
It was his house.
He was staying on top of the offices.
He say, there are 20-- 20 youngsters like you from Rhodes Island.
They are 20 people there.
We couldn't believe.
We were so overjoyed to hear that there's some other people were from Rhodes Island like us in there.
And then, you know, quickly, he say, go upstairs.
Find yourself a room.
You can stay here until, you know, you find the relatives and whatever it is because we got all this made-- you know, ready for you.
And we went upstairs.
And we find, you know, all the other people.
There was specially a man that now, later on, become my brother-in-law.
But at the time, he was just a friend from Rhodes Island.
And he was very, very friendly with my eldest sister.
They used to play cards in Rhodes Island before the war.
He was married.
But he lost his wife and his children.
His name is Giuseppe Malel.
We find them, all the other ones-- they were thin, they were fed, whatever.
But you can recognize them.
This man, he was so thin, so different.
And then, I say-- I said to the girls, who else is here.
Giuseppe Malel, that is him, this, and this, and that.
Say, where is Giuseppe Malel?
He say, I'm Giuseppe Malel.
You are Giuseppe-- you are not Giuseppe Malel, because we cannot believe it was him.
His face was completely possible.
His hair is grey.
And he was another person, unbelievable.
Many people, you know, they still-- you know-- they-- you know, you can recognize them a little bit.
But this person was unbelievable, the way he has changed.
So we stayed there, you know, in this community for nearly one year.

And then, you know, by the time we're there, you know, there the magazines and the newspapers, and the radio was announcing every day that Jewish refugees from Rome, from Italy, from Rhodes Island, are start coming back from the concentration camp.
Among the other, you know, people, there were our names.
So now everybody-- we had these two brothers in the Belgian Congo.
And like us, all the other 20 people, they were there.
Some, you know, came to Africa.
Some, they had relative in America.
And they start, you know, inquiring, and sending messages through the Red Cross, because, at the time, the post they were unbelievable.
You can send a letter and it arrive now in two days.
It was maybe two months before you can get.
But to give us courage, they were sending, you know, by-- you know, by telegram through the Red Cross, even money, my thing.
And one thing we will never forget in our life, never, we are very, very grateful to the American nation, you know, the American Jews.
They did whatever they do, because in their place, they were quicker to send us help.
Our crowd, you know, here, in Belgian Congo and in South Africa, there were so many Jews that were trying to help.
But they would find difficulty in the arrangement.
But the American people, straight away they form, you know, society, like the [NON-ENGLISH].
They call the Joint.
You know, they were very, very-- very, very quick to send us help in kind and in money.
And also, you know, the American soldiers that they were Jews, you know, they were trying-- you know, they were in [NON-ENGLISH], nearby Bologna and other places few kilometers away.
Whenever they'd leave, those American Jewish boys, they will come to the rabbi-- you know, to the rabbi house to visit the refugee, you know, girls.
They were really wonderful.
That's why, you know, in my heart, I keep-- as I keep Israel, I keep America in my heart, like my-- you know-- I can't explain.
You see, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because they were the only people-- not only that, they were sending-- you know, they had our names.
Every month, you know, they were sending, individually, to each one, a parcel of clothing, you know.
So much so that we had too much, because we were poor.
And you know what poor it is, because the many poor in Italy at the time after the war, you know, they were just going from place to place, from house to house, to collect the charity.
And we were so sorry for them.
Sometimes, you know, when we had enough of our clothing, we would make a little parcel at night.
And we'll throw-- And the house of the rabbi was on the second floor.
We will throw through the window, you know, a parcel.
Whoever found it, found it, the surplus that we didn't need it, you know.
Because one poor, you know, understand the other poor.
Like you see, you know, in the films, some beggars, you know, they got a piece of bread, they share it.
But when you are rich and you have too much, you don't like to share to other people.
After Bologna, you know, my brothers that they were in Africa, they heard that we were there, that they are also trying to send money, and the help, and whatever it is.
And then, you know, they send-- and they make, you know, the arrangement for us to go to the Belgian Congo, where they live themselves.
But it took, you know, to make all this paper.
And you have to have, you know, like somebody to sponsor you to come to Belgian Congo at the time, like in South Africa and all this.
So it elapsed, you know, from September 1945 till 1946 December.
We arrive in the Belgian Congo.
But before that we arrive, you know, there, we have to go-- we have to go to Rome and to take the passport.
To take the passport, because only there they give passport to be made for desperate people.
We had the passport, and the ticket, and all collected.
We went by train to Belgian-- to Brussels.

Because, at the time, you know, after the war, Brussels was the only country, as yet, you know, this colony, who had, you know, these yellow-- yellow-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, yellow sickness vaccination.
They didn't exist nowhere in the world.
And also, to have vaccination against the sickness of the sleep.
You know, in the-- a long time ago, in the Belgian Congo, there were all these tsetse flies and all these that we bring you disease, you know.
You know, you can sleep.
And you can sleep forever, you die.
So when you come there, it was, you know, it was the compulsory to have those vaccination-- one against the yellow fever and tsetse fly above the [FRENCH].
They call it [FRENCH] in French.
It means the sickness of sleeping.
So, once, we have to-- and then, you know, not only you have to be vaccinated, you have to--
[BACKGROUND NOISE] It want to be vaccinated, you see.
And-- we stayed there.
Not only when you had this vaccination, you have to know that the vaccination has been positive-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Vaccination has been positive.
